data.title: European indexes pull back after touching fresh high
data.description:
London (UKX) -1.11% to 9,698, UK gilt yields surged and the pound weakened following reports that Chancellor Rachel Reeves may drop plans to raise income tax in the Nov. 26 budget.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.89% at 23,833. 

France (CAC:IND) -0.64% to 8,179. France inflation drops to 0.9% in October

In other parts of Europe, Spain’s inflation rises to 3.1% in October.

Slovakia’s economy expanded 0.9% Y/Y in Q3.

Hungary’s construction output climbed by 17.6% Y/Y in September.

Italy's 10-year government bond yield increased to 3.45%, the highest since October 2025.

Sweden’s unemployment rate climbed to 8.9% in October.

Finland’s annual inflation rate fell 0.2% in October 

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) dropped 0.59% to 580.8, marking a second straight session of losses. Investors cautioned over potential overvaluation in the AI sector and the risk of a bubble. Bitcoin fell toward $96,000 on Friday, marking a six month low amid a broad selloff in risk assets and reduced expectations for a US Federal Reserve rate cut in December.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury was up 2 basis points to 4.13%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 6 basis points to 4.49%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was up 2 basis points to 2.71%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Blackstone hires ex-Morgan Stanley rainmaker Franck Petitgas - report
data.description:
• Blackstone (BX) has appointed former Morgan Stanley (MS) rainmaker Franck Petitgas to a top role in Europe as it prepares to invest $500 billion there over the next 10 years, Bloomberg News reported.
  • Petitgas, who used to run Morgan Stanley’s investment bank and was once considered a candidate to lead the Wall Street firm, will join Blackstone as a senior managing director in January, the report said.

data.title: Bavarian Nordic A/S reports Q3 results; updates FY outlook
data.description:
• Bavarian Nordic A/S press release (BVNKF): Q3 Revenue of DKK 1.79B (+31.6% Y/Y).
  • Net profit of DKK 1.09B.
  • 2026 outlook While a full financial guidance for 2026 is not provided yet, the Company has confirmed orders in Public Preparedness of approximately DKK 1,100 million, which includes revenue from the ongoing contract for freeze-dried smallpox vaccine with the US government and revenue under the new Joint Procurement Agreement with the European Commission’s Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority. As previously communicated, the annual base business in Public Preparedness is expected at DKK 1,500 – 2,000 million, excluding revenue from private markets (US + Germany) and impact from outbreaks, and in Travel Health, an average annual growth of 10-12% is expected in the period 2023-2027.

data.title: Sonova Holding Non-GAAP EPS of CHF 3.56, revenue of CHF 1.82B; reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Sonova Holding AG press release (SONVF): 1H Non-GAAP EPS of CHF 3.56.
  • Revenue of CHF 1.82B (-0.5% Y/Y), up 4.9% in local currencies.
  •  Organic growth was 4.5%, and acquisitions in the reporting period, along with the full-year effect of prior-year acquisitions, contributed 0.4% to total sales growth.
  • In local currencies, normalized EBITA rose by 16.0%, reaching CHF 316.1 million and representing a margin increase of 1.8 percentage points, reflecting strong operating leverage. 
  • The Group confirms the outlook for the 2025/26 financial year and continues to expect consolidated sales to increase by 5-9% and normalized EBITA to grow by 14-18% in the 2025/26 financial year, both measured at constant exchange rates.

data.title: Baidu shares slide as new AI model fails to impress investors
data.description:
• Shares in Baidu (BIDU) fell on Friday as the newest version of its artificial intelligence model underwhelmed investors.
  • The stock dropped as much as 9.8% Friday in Hong Kong following the unveiling of Ernie 5.0 at a conference.
  • Despite the stock’s more than 40% gain this year, investors were disappointed by the latest flagship update, which lacked a standout wow factor.
  • The M100—an inference-focused chip—is set to be launched in early 2026, while the M300, capable of both training and inference, is slated for early 2027, according to a Reuters report.

data.title: Vallourec S.A. reports Q3 results
data.description:
• Vallourec S.A. press release (VLOUF): Q3 Group EBITDA of €210 million, up 12% sequentially, in line with midpoint of guidance
  • Group EBITDA margin was strong at 23%, the highest level since Q1 2024
  • Q4 2025 Group EBITDA expected to range between €195 million and €225 million
  • Confirm expected improvement in EBITDA in H2 2025 vs. H1 2025

data.title: Nagarro SE reports Q3 results; reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Nagarro SE press release (NGRRF): Q3 Revenue of €254.57M (+4.8% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of revenue was 17.3% in Q3 2025, compared with 12.2% in Q1 2025 and 12.1% in Q2 2025.
  • Gross margin increased to 33.1% in Q3 2025 as compared to 31.5% in Q3 2024.
  • Outlook: "We maintain the expectations expressed in the half-yearly financial report."

data.title: Spire raises dividend by 5.1% to $0.825 a share
data.description:
• Spire (SR) declares $0.825/share quarterly dividend, 5.1% increase from prior dividend of $0.785.
  • Forward yield 3.7%
  • Payable Jan. 5; to shareholders of record Dec. 11; ex-div Dec. 11.
  • The company raised its quarterly dividend by 5.1% after paying a quarterly dividend of $0.785 in each of the previous 4 quarters.
  • See SR Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Fed's Kashkari did not back last rate cut, undecided on December - report
data.description:
Minneapolis Federal Reserve President Neel Kashkari did not support the Fed's interest rate cut last month, he told Bloomberg News in an interview, but he's undecided on the next policy move.

"The anecdotal evidence and the data we got just implied to me underlying resilience in economic activity, more than I had expected," he said, noting that this argued for a pause in the Fed's October meeting.

Kashkari said available data since then have suggested "more of the same" for the economy. As for the Fed's meeting next month, he said, "I can make a case depending on how the data goes to cut, I can make a case to hold, and we'll have to see."

To note, Kashkari doesn't hold a vote on the Federal Open Market Committee's rate decisions this year, but he participates in its deliberations.

Some Fed policymakers have also expressed skepticism over the need for a rate cut in December.

Boston Fed President Susan Collins said she'd be hesitant to ease policy further, absent evidence of a notable labor market deterioration. Collins is a voting member in the FOMC this year.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, another FOMC voting member, indicated he was reluctant to cut rates near term due to the lack of inflation data during the government shutdown. "Medium-run, I'm not hawkish on rates. When it's foggy, let's just be a little careful and slow down."

Market participants are currently pricing in 52% odds of a 25-basis point rate cut in December, according to CME's FedWatch tool. A month ago, the odds were around 94%.

data.title: China's BYD to phase out in-house D-Chain payments as supplier pressure mounts
data.description:
China’s electric vehicle giant BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) is shifting away from its in-house payment system called Dilian, which it used to pay suppliers through promissory notes. Instead, the EV maker plans to use commercial paper or bank notes for supplier payments, according to a Reuters report, citing sources familiar with the matter.

The supply chain finance tool called 'D-Chain' was launched in 2018 and allowed the company to keep costs low and speed up production; however, suppliers often faced delays of up to 127 days, above the Chinese industry average of 108 days.

The adjustment is being piloted among certain small and medium-sized suppliers. Due to its broad impact, communication is mostly conducted one-on-one and has not been formally announced, a local Chinese media report pointed out. Many suppliers have not yet received notice, particularly those who halted supply and still hold D-Chain claims.

The shift likely comes amid regulatory pressure to shorten payment times and improve transparency in the auto industry. China’s new rules now require automakers to pay suppliers within 60 days and limit the use of non-cash payments, though loopholes remain.

BYD told Reuters that its Dilian system falls within regulatory guidelines and it has sped up payments to suppliers this year, but it did not address queries about the move away from in-house notes.

BYD Company did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.
